The senior international staff member continues the behaviour — two more national staff report it to Sione. The training budget reallocation sets a precedent.
options.delayed_impact: Leila files a formal duty of care complaint with HopeForward HQ. Three national staff turnover in a single month. An exit interview report cites 'leadership failure on staff wellbeing and discrimination' as the primary reason.

options.label: Evacuate Leila, formally address the discrimination complaint, restore training budget
options.description_summary: Formally suspend Northern Coast operations and evacuate Leila's team to the Talio hub. Open a formal HR investigation into the discrimination complaint (protecting Tevi from retaliation). Reverse the training budget reallocation — make a case to HQ for the cost of the commitment. Set up a confidential staff wellbeing check-in process.
options.chs_learning_note: Addressing all three staff management issues in parallel demonstrates that CHS 8 is lived at leadership level — not just policy on paper.
options.consequence_summary: Leila and her team reach Talio safely and thank Maya directly. The discrimination investigation is conducted confidentially and fairly. The senior international staff member receives a final written warning. Training budgets are restored. Staff retention in month 6 is the highest in HopeForward Kailani's history.
options.delayed_impact: Three national staff who had been considering leaving stay because of how leadership handled the discrimination case. Leila re-joins the programme in month 6 after a supported rest period.

options.label: Evacuate Leila; handle discrimination complaint informally; don't reverse training budget
options.description_summary: Evacuate Leila — her safety is non-negotiable. Speak privately to the senior international staff member but don't open a formal investigation. Accept the training reallocation as a done deal.
requirements_missed: 8.5 — Tevi's complaint not through formal channel
options.chs_learning_note: Partial action on duty of care is better than inaction, but informal handling of discrimination complaints leaves the discriminatory behaviour unaddressed and unrecorded.
options.consequence_summary: Leila is safe. But the senior international staff member, not formally disciplined, continues the behaviour with increased subtlety. Tevi submits a formal complaint to HQ directly two months later — bypassing Maya entirely because the informal approach didn't produce visible change.
options.delayed_impact: HQ launches a global HR audit of HopeForward Kailani after receiving Tevi's complaint. The informal handling of the original report is flagged as a management failure.

options.label: Restructure team management: wellbeing policy, staff forum, formal HR process for all three issues
options.description_summary: All of 8B, plus: use the moment to establish a permanent staff wellbeing policy — monthly check-ins, a confidential staff concerns hotline, a national staff advisory forum with real power. Document all three HR cases and their resolutions in HopeForward's policy review.
options.chs_learning_note: Beyond resolving individual incidents, CHS 8.1 and 8.7 call for systemic approaches — organisational culture and fair HR management. The three concurrent issues are also a diagnosis of system gaps.
options.consequence_summary: All three issues are resolved with appropriate processes and documented outcomes. The new national staff advisory forum surfaces two programme improvement ideas within a month. HopeForward Kailani has zero involuntary staff turnover in month 6.
options.delayed_impact: HopeForward global uses the Kailani staff wellbeing model in its updated HR guidance. Three other country programmes adopt the national staff advisory forum structure.

scenes.narrative:
Maya has three browser tabs open and is not looking at any of them.

On her phone: a WhatsApp thread from Leila Hassan in the Northern Coast, four unread messages. The most recent, sent at 3:17 am: “Day 3. No food in the house tonight. I’m scared. What should I do?”

On her desk: a folded handwritten note, placed there by a colleague that morning. Tevi Moli, national programme officer, has written it in Kailani first, then translated it into English. He names a senior international staff member. He describes a pattern — not a single incident — of demeaning comments about Kailani nationals, and a practice of assigning lower-skilled tasks exclusively to national staff. The note ends: “I am not writing this through the complaint box because I am afraid of what happens after.”

The third thing is an email from Carlos, three weeks ago: the skills training budget has been reallocated to cover a logistics shortfall. Maya found out fifteen minutes ago, in a routine check-in, when one team member mentioned it in passing while discussing professional goals. “Oh — I assumed you knew,” she said. Three staff members hold contracts specifying skills development support in months 5 and 6.

Where HopeForward established the protection_framework_active flag in Decision 4, a formal misconduct reporting mechanism already exists — Tevi would have a clear, documented pathway. Where that framework was never built, he has written a handwritten note and is waiting to see whether it disappears.

Three issues. One morning. One person deciding how to respond to all three at once.

scenes.title: Three Failures, One Month
scenes.narrative_summary: Leila's team member breaks down in the field after a 14-hour checkpoint standoff and is medically evacuated. Tevi, after the informal conversation with his senior colleague 'changed nothing,' contacts HQ directly. Three national staff resign in the same week. Carlos, reviewing the month's metrics, notes that productivity dropped 30% in the Northern Coast. Exit interviews tell a consistent story: leadership chose operational metrics over people.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Staff are not resources to be managed around programme delivery — they are the programme. Every failure under CHS 8 produces a direct cost: in programme quality, in retention, and in the dignity of the people we employ.
scenes.chs_quote: Maintain a safe and inclusive working environment, taking measures to protect the safety, security and well-being and dignity of all staff and volunteers. (CHS 8.2)
scenes.image_prompt: Northern Coast field site, day 15. Leila Hassan sits alone in the team’s remaining shelter, phone in hand, WhatsApp thread visible — her last message unanswered for 18 hours. An empty camp chair beside her marks where her evacuated colleague sat. In the distance, a militia checkpoint blocks the road. Inset left: Tevi Moli at his desk in Port Hana, expression closed, two colleagues visible in background, one watching. Inset right: three printed employment contracts on a desk, the 'skills development support' clause circled in pencil with a question mark. Fading light. 16-bit pixel art scene, Pacific island humanitarian setting, grey-ochre palette, exhaustion and institutional absence.
scenes.narrative:
Leila’s messages had been arriving since day two. “Checkpoint again. Haven’t moved since morning — are there any instructions?” Then, at 3 am: “Day 3. No food in the house tonight. I’m scared. What should I do?” Maya forwarded both to Carlos. The response, the following afternoon: “Security situation is temporary. HQ wants Northern Coast coverage maintained.”

No evacuation order was issued. On day 14, one team member experienced a severe stress reaction after a twelve-hour checkpoint standoff. He was medically evacuated to Nairobi that evening. Leila continued alone for another week.

Tevi’s informal conversation with the senior international staff member produced no visible change — his exact words to Sione, two weeks later. Two other national staff described the same pattern: demeaning remarks about Kailani nationals, lower-skilled assignments reserved exclusively for national staff. Neither duty_of_care_upheld nor protection_framework_active had been established — staff had no expectation that leadership would treat wellbeing seriously or that a formal reporting channel would be acted on. None of them believed reporting would produce a different outcome.

Carlos’s training reallocation was documented as a logistics necessity. Three contracts specified skills development support in months 5 and 6. That commitment was overridden without consultation and not restored.

Exit interviews three weeks later read identically: “Leadership chose operational metrics over people.” The phrase names it precisely: CHS 8.1 requires a culture of quality and accountability at leadership level. Without it, 8.2, 8.3, 8.4, 8.5, 8.6, and 8.7 all follow it into failure.

scenes.title: The Call That Mattered
scenes.narrative_summary: Leila arrives at Talio hub at 9pm. She calls Maya. 'Thank you for listening.' The discrimination investigation is hard and uncomfortable — a well-liked international staff member, evidence reviewed carefully, outcome communicated. Tevi receives a written update within two weeks. Three national staff who had started job searches decide to stay. By month 6, the team has the best retention rate in HopeForward's Asia-Pacific region.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Duty of care, code of conduct enforcement, and professional development support are not three separate obligations — they are three aspects of the same thing: treating your staff as fully human.
scenes.chs_quote: Take timely, appropriate actions to address misconduct of all staff and volunteers, in line with recognised good practice. (CHS 8.6)
scenes.image_prompt: Talio coordination hub, 9 pm. Leila Hassan sits at a table in the common room, phone to her ear, relief visible — one team member sleeps in a chair behind her, the other accepts tea from a hub volunteer. Through the window: the safe Talio road. Inset left: a formal HR investigation folder open on a desk, support person’s name card beside it, Tevi Moli’s written statement clipped inside. Inset right: Carlos Reyes signs a memo acknowledging the training budget reallocation overrode a contract commitment, Maya Chen watching. Warm light. 16-bit pixel art scene, Pacific island humanitarian setting, greens and warm ochre, sense of systems working and dignity restored.
scenes.narrative:
Maya reads Leila’s 3 am message — “Day 3. No food in the house tonight. I’m scared. What should I do?” — and calls immediately. Within four hours, the formal security-suspension protocol is activated. Leila and her team reach Talio by 9 pm. Her call to Maya that evening is short: “Thank you for listening.” She means it.

The discrimination investigation is opened formally and confidentially, at a pace Tevi helps set. He is offered a support person from the first conversation; his account is treated as credible and documented in full. The senior international staff member receives a final written warning after evidence is reviewed by a regional case officer. The outcome is communicated to Tevi before it is entered in any system. His message to Sione, one week later: “I didn’t expect the process to feel fair. It did.”

Three staff training budgets are restored in full after Maya presents the contractual obligation to HQ. Carlos acknowledges in writing that the reallocation overrode a contract commitment and that skills development entitlements take precedence.

Where protection_framework_active was established in Decision 4, investigation protocols were already documented — the process ran more smoothly. Either way, duty_of_care_upheld and misconduct_addressed_formally are now set: staff entering month 6 know the system works.

Zero involuntary staff departures in month 6. CHS 8.1, 8.2, 8.3, 8.4, 8.5, 8.6, and 8.7 all met.

scenes.title: Safe But Not Protected
scenes.narrative_summary: Leila is in Talio and resting. Her team is relieved. But in the Port Hana office, the senior international staff member's behaviour continues — more carefully, but consistently. Tevi talks to two other national staff colleagues, who confirm they've experienced the same. Two months later, a formal complaint to HQ triggers the investigation that should have happened in month 5. The informal approach cost two months and significant staff distress.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Evacuating a team member from a security risk is essential. But leaving a discrimination complaint informally handled means the harm continues. Both are CHS 8 obligations.
scenes.chs_quote: Ensure all staff and volunteers understand and adhere to a code of conduct, which, at a minimum, prohibits any form of exploitation, abuse, harassment, or discrimination. (CHS 8.4)
scenes.image_prompt: Port Hana field office, two weeks after Leila’s evacuation. The senior international staff member works at his desk, composed, while two national staff — including Tevi Moli — work in silence in the background, exchanging a glance. On Maya’s desk in the foreground: Leila’s formal security-suspension file stamped ‘Resolved,’ but beside it Tevi’s handwritten note has no response attached. A printed training schedule shows the skills development sessions still blocked in red: ‘Budget: Reallocated.’ Split mood — one crisis resolved, two unaddressed. 16-bit pixel art scene, Pacific island humanitarian setting, muted ochre palette, institutional unease.
scenes.narrative:
Leila’s voice note from Talio arrives at 11 pm: “We made it. I’m okay — just exhausted.” Her team members sleep through until noon. The security suspension is documented and filed.

In Port Hana, the conversation with the senior international staff member is private, brief, and unrecorded. No formal process opens. The behaviour resumes within two weeks — more carefully, but consistently. Tevi raises it with two colleagues; both confirm they have experienced the same pattern of demeaning remarks and task assignments. None of them sees any visible outcome from the conversation.

The training budget reallocation stands. Three staff members’ contractual skills development entitlements are not restored. Carlos does not revisit the decision.

Where protection_framework_active was established in Decision 4, a formal reporting pathway existed for Tevi — but an informal leadership response gave no signal that the pathway would be used. It remained available and unused. duty_of_care_upheld is set for Leila’s safety, but Tevi’s experience reveals its limits: physical safety and dignity at work are both CHS 8 obligations.

Two months later Tevi files a formal complaint directly with HQ. The investigation names the informal handling of a code of conduct breach as a management failure: an informal response to discrimination does not meet the standard of CHS 8.4 or 8.6, and without a clear leadership culture (CHS 8.1), the formal pathway was available but not trusted. CHS 8.2 met; 8.1, 8.3, 8.4, 8.5, 8.6, and 8.7 missed.

scenes.title: From Crisis to Culture
scenes.narrative_summary: The three concurrent issues become the foundation for something different. The national staff advisory forum meets for the first time and immediately identifies three programme improvements that international staff hadn't seen. The wellbeing check-ins reveal that two other staff members were struggling silently. The discrimination case is resolved fairly and sets a precedent that is widely known and respected across the team.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Individual HR crises, when handled with systemic thinking, reveal and fix structural gaps. The best response to three simultaneous failures is not just to fix them — it is to build the systems that prevent them from recurring.
scenes.chs_quote: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure that human resources are managed effectively in a fair, non-discriminatory and transparent manner. (CHS 8.7)
scenes.image_prompt: HopeForward Port Hana office, three weeks after the crisis. The national staff advisory forum sits around a table — eight national staff members, Sione Taufa facilitating, a whiteboard showing two flagged programme improvements. Maya Chen sits at the back, listening. On the desk behind her: three employment contract folders with ‘Training Budget Restored — HR Approved’ sticky notes. A wall calendar shows Leila Hassan’s return date circled in green. Small inset: Tevi Moli accepts a written outcome letter, expression composed and dignified. Warm, functional atmosphere. 16-bit pixel art scene, Pacific island humanitarian setting, greens and ochre, sense of culture built from crisis.
scenes.narrative:
Leila’s voice note from Talio arrives at 7 pm: “We’re safe. Thank you for making the call.” The formal security suspension is documented and shared with HQ as a reference model.

Tevi’s discrimination complaint is investigated formally and confidentially, with a support person present at every stage. The senior international staff member receives a final written warning; the outcome is communicated to Tevi before it enters any record. His message to Sione: “I didn’t expect to be treated with this much care. It matters.”

Three training budgets are restored in full. Carlos acknowledges in writing that contractual skills development entitlements cannot be reallocated without HR sign-off — a precedent, not just a correction.

The national staff advisory forum meets three weeks later. In its opening session it surfaces two programme improvements international staff had not seen. Two staff members who had been struggling in silence come forward through the new monthly wellbeing check-ins.

With duty_of_care_upheld established and protection_framework_active confirmed, staff describe the same shift: concerns are expected to be raised and will be heard. That expectation is a culture, not a policy on paper.

Zero involuntary departures in month 6. HopeForward global adopts the Kailani model. CHS 8.1, 8.2, 8.3, 8.4, 8.5, 8.6, and 8.7 all met.

scenes.title: Decision 9 — How Do You Manage the Remaining Resources Ethically?

chs_commitment.title: People and communities can expect that resources are managed ethically and responsibly.

all_requirements.9.1: Ensure adequate capacity and resources to meet the organisation's commitments.
all_requirements.9.2: Manage financial resources responsibly in line with recognised good practices.
all_requirements.9.3: Ensure fundraising, resource mobilisation, and fund allocations are ethical and do not compromise the organisation's commitments and values.
all_requirements.9.4: Manage and use resources to achieve their intended purpose, minimising waste and the impact on the environment.
all_requirements.9.5: Identify, prevent and manage risks at all levels of the organisation, including corruption, fraud, misuse of resources and conflicts of interest, and take appropriate action if these are identified.
all_requirements.9.6: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure that resources are managed efficiently, effectively and ethically.

scenes.situation_summary: Month 6. The programme is ending. Toma, HopeForward's Finance Manager, brings Maya three resource issues: (A) A budget underspend of $47,000 exists — due to a procurement process that came in under budget and one position that was never filled. The donor's agreement technically requires unspent funds to be returned. (B) Toma has discovered that the procurement manager who handled the supply chain in month 1 has a family connection to one of the three winning suppliers — a connection that was not disclosed. The procurement decision may be fine, but the undisclosed relationship is a conflict of interest. (C) HQ has asked Maya to use $12,000 of the remaining budget for a 'visibility campaign' — branded materials, a printed report, and a professional photoshoot — for a new funding pitch. No beneficiary consent forms exist for any of the community photos that would be used.

context_flag_effects.donor_transparent_engagement: Donor already trusts HopeForward — transparent discussion of the underspend is more likely to result in reallocation approval.
context_flag_effects.community_informed: Communities understand their rights; they are more likely to expect and demand consent for use of their images.
context_flag_effects.data_protection_in_place: Data protection norms are established — consent for image use is understood as an extension of this.

scenes.image_prompt: Toma Faleolo, Pacific Island man in his 40s, formal office shirt and lanyard, stands at the meeting table in the HopeForward Port Hana office. He has placed three documents in precise order in front of Maya Chen: left — a budget spreadsheet with ‘$47,000 underspend’ highlighted in yellow; centre — a procurement audit note with a supplier name circled in red and ‘undisclosed relationship’ handwritten in the margin; right — a printout of an HQ email with a community family photograph embedded in the body, subject line ‘Visibility Campaign — $12,000 remaining funds.’ Maya sits opposite, reading the third document, expression attentive and careful. Late afternoon light through slatted coastal blinds. 16-bit pixel art scene, HopeForward field office, Port Hana, Pacific island humanitarian setting, ochre-grey palette, professional integrity under pressure.

options.label: Return underspend; quietly note the conflict of interest; approve HQ visibility campaign
options.description_summary: Return the $47k to the donor as required. Write up the conflict of interest in an internal note but take no formal action. Approve the $12k visibility campaign and instruct the team to gather some community photos quickly.
requirements_missed: 9.3 — visibility campaign without consent violates ethics
9.4 — underspend returned rather than reallocated to community benefit
9.5 — conflict of interest not formally managed
9.6 — no coherent resource management approach demonstrated
options.chs_learning_note: Technically returning funds and letting ethical issues slide is not ethical resource management. CHS 9 requires active stewardship and integrity at every level.
options.consequence_summary: The underspend is returned. The conflict of interest surfaces in a routine external audit 6 months later — and the fact that it was noted internally but not acted on makes it worse. Community photos appear in HopeForward's fundraising materials without consent — two community members recognise themselves and complain.
options.delayed_impact: A donor review is triggered by the audit finding. HopeForward's conflict of interest policy is found to be inadequate. The photography complaint is escalated to a global PSEA review.

options.label: Negotiate underspend reallocation; formally investigate conflict of interest; decline unethical visibility campaign
options.description_summary: Approach the donor transparently about reallocating the $47k to unmet community needs (KAN nutrition gap, community early warning system upgrade, KCV capacity). Formally investigate the procurement conflict of interest with Toma. Decline HQ's visibility campaign request — or restructure it with full community consent and benefit-sharing for participants.
options.chs_learning_note: Ethical resource management means using every available resource for the intended purpose — including negotiating with donors for community benefit — and refusing to compromise values under institutional pressure.
options.consequence_summary: The donor agrees to reallocate $35k to KAN nutrition and $12k to the early warning system upgrade. The conflict of interest investigation finds no fraud but results in a revised procurement policy. The visibility campaign is redesigned with community consent — participants are credited and receive printed copies of their photos.
options.delayed_impact: The reallocated funding directly reaches 800 children under 5 through KAN's nutrition programme. The revised procurement policy is adopted globally by HopeForward. The consent-based photography model is shared as a good practice example.

options.label: Reallocate underspend without donor approval; formally address conflict of interest; decline visibility campaign
options.description_summary: Reallocate the $47k directly to KAN and the early warning system without first seeking donor approval — it is the right use of the money. Investigate the conflict of interest formally. Decline the visibility campaign.
requirements_addressed: 9.3 — visibility campaign refused on ethical grounds
requirements_missed: 9.2 — unilateral reallocation without donor consent violates financial management rules
options.chs_learning_note: Acting with good intent while bypassing financial management rules creates accountability risk that can undermine the entire programme. CHS 9.2 and 9.6 require both ethical intent AND proper process.
options.consequence_summary: The reallocation reaches the right people. But six months later, a routine audit flags the unilateral reallocation as a financial management breach. Toma is held responsible. The conflict of interest investigation finding is overshadowed by the audit breach.
options.delayed_impact: HopeForward must repay the $47k from its own reserves. Toma is formally censured despite having flagged the issue in good faith. The lesson: good outcomes do not retrospectively justify improper process.

options.label: Return underspend; ignore conflict of interest; fully approve HQ visibility campaign as-is
options.description_summary: Return the $47k as required. Leave the conflict of interest in a filing cabinet. Approve the full $12k visibility campaign, photos included.
requirements_missed: 9.4 — resources not achieving best community benefit
options.chs_learning_note: Compliance with the letter of financial requirements while failing their spirit is not ethical resource management. CHS 9 demands active stewardship and integrity, not only rule-following.
options.consequence_summary: Everything appears clean on paper. But within a year: the conflict of interest surfaces in an external audit; community members file a complaint about unconsented image use; and HQ's visibility campaign is later cited in a sector report on exploitative humanitarian photography.
options.delayed_impact: HopeForward appears in a Guardian article headlined 'When NGO Fundraising Exploits the Communities It Claims to Serve.' The Kailani programme is cited by name. Donor relationship severely damaged.

scenes.narrative:
Toma Faleolo closes the office door and places three documents on the table in front of Maya. He is precise about the order.

First: the budget reconciliation. Month-6 underspend: $47,000. The supply chain procurement came in $28,000 under estimate; a field coordinator post was unfilled for four months. Clause 14.3 of the donor agreement requires return of unspent funds. “I’ve modelled a reallocation case,” Toma says. “KAN’s nutrition gap, the early warning system upgrade, KCV community outreach. The community benefit is real. But the donor has to approve it.” He sets a two-page reallocation request on top of the spreadsheet. “This could be answered in 48 hours.”

Second: a procurement relationship he found last week. The supplier who won the month-1 medical supplies contract has a documented family connection to the procurement manager who led that process. “I’ve reviewed the scoring sheets twice,” Toma says. “The selection looks sound. But the relationship was never disclosed. That is a conflict of interest under our policy. I have to report it formally.”

Third: the HQ email, which he sets down carefully. Regional office is requesting $12,000 from the remaining programme budget for a visibility campaign — a printed donor report, branded materials, and a professional photoshoot in an affected community. The email includes a photograph of a family from Manu Camp with the note ‘Something like this.’ “HQ is not asking for anything unusual by sector norms,” Toma says. “But I do not have consent documentation for that photograph, or for any community images in our files. And I want you to be clear on the fund-use language before you respond to them.”

He looks at Maya evenly. He is not telling her what to decide. He is making sure she has what he has.

Where donor_transparent_engagement is established, the reallocation request already has a foundation — the donor trusts the relationship and is likely to respond quickly. Where community_informed is active, Manu Camp residents understand their rights over their images; a consent failure will be noticed.

CHS 9.1 through 9.6 are all at stake in this meeting.

scenes.title: Technically Compliant, Ethically Failing
scenes.narrative_summary: The $47k is returned — a line on a spreadsheet that satisfies the contract. The conflict of interest note sits in a filing cabinet. Community photos appear on HopeForward's website and fundraising brochure within a month. Six months later: an external audit finds the undisclosed procurement relationship; two community members in Kailani see themselves on a donation appeal website and contact KCV to complain. Neither consented. HopeForward's global communications team is briefed to respond.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Ethical resource management requires active stewardship — not just technical compliance. The spirit of CHS 9 is about using every resource for the communities it is intended to serve.
scenes.chs_quote: Identify, prevent and manage risks at all levels of the organisation, including corruption, fraud, misuse of resources and conflicts of interest. (CHS 9.5)
scenes.image_prompt: HopeForward Port Hana office, end of month 6. Left panel: Toma Faleolo sits at his desk, a wire-transfer confirmation on screen, expression neutral — the $47,000 returned, a filing cabinet drawer half-open behind him, one folder labelled ‘Month 6 Internal Notes.’ Centre panel: a printed HopeForward donor report spread open on a table, cover photograph showing a Kailani family from Manu Camp — two adults, one child, unaware they are the cover image of an international fundraising document. Right panel: a community noticeboard in Manu Camp, a woman pointing at the printed page with the same photo — KCV’s Filomena Vaka stands beside her, phone out, taking notes. 16-bit pixel art scene, Pacific island humanitarian setting, muted ochre-grey palette, quiet institutional failure made visible.
scenes.narrative:
The $47,000 is returned to the donor by wire transfer within 72 hours. Toma files the confirmation without comment. His three-page conflict of interest note — careful, documented, precise — goes into a folder labelled ‘Month 6 Internal Notes.’ No formal process is opened. The procurement manager is not informed. The issue has been put in front of Maya; she has made her decision.

The visibility campaign is approved at $12,000. A Suva-based agency is commissioned within a week. The Manu Camp family photograph appears on the cover of HopeForward’s printed donor report and on the donation appeal page of the organisation’s website.

In October, a community member in Manu Camp sees her family’s image on that page. She has not consented. She contacts KCV. A second community member reports independently the following week. Where community_informed had been established, both individuals would have understood their consent rights before the campaign launched — and would have raised concerns earlier, with greater institutional confidence.

Six months post-programme, a routine external audit surfaces the undisclosed procurement relationship. The internal note — written, filed, unacted — makes the finding worse: documented internal awareness of a conflict of interest, without formal management action, represents a governance failure more serious than the original omission. An HQ donor review is convened. The photography complaints, escalated to the global PSEA unit, run in parallel.

CHS 9.2 partially met. 9.3 missed — community images used in fundraising without consent. 9.4 missed — $47,000 returned to donor rather than reallocated to community benefit. 9.5 missed — conflict of interest documented but not managed. 9.6 missed — no coherent ethical stewardship demonstrated. 9.1 missed — the staffing gap that created the underspend was never assessed.

scenes.title: Every Dollar for Its Purpose
scenes.narrative_summary: The donor accepts the reallocation proposal in 24 hours, impressed by the transparent request. $35k reaches KAN nutrition programmes within a week — 800 children under 5 receive supplementary feeding for an additional month. The conflict of interest investigation is documented and results in a stronger procurement policy. The visibility campaign, redesigned with community consent and benefit-sharing, wins a sector award for ethical communications.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Ethical resource management means negotiating with donors for community benefit, not just returning funds. It means confronting uncomfortable truths about procurement. And it means protecting communities' dignity even in fundraising.
scenes.chs_quote: Manage and use resources to achieve their intended purpose, minimising waste and the impact on the environment. (CHS 9.4)
scenes.image_prompt: Three moments, one month later. Left panel: Toma Faleolo and Maya Chen at a desk, a donor reallocation approval email visible on screen — a spreadsheet beside it shows ‘$35,000 → KAN Nutrition, $12,000 → Early Warning System Upgrade.’ Centre panel: a KAN community nutrition site, Dr. Ana Tuivaga and two health workers distributing supplementary food packages to mothers with young children, a hand-painted sign reading ‘Month 7 Nutrition Support.’ Right panel: a community meeting in Manu Camp — a HopeForward staff member presents a printed consent form to seated community members, small photographs on the table showing what images would be used and how; Filomena Vaka of KCV sits alongside, observing. Warm, purposeful light. 16-bit pixel art scene, Pacific island humanitarian setting, warm green and ochre palette, ethical systems working.
scenes.narrative:
The donor reallocation request is sent at 9 am. By 11 am the following day — where donor_transparent_engagement is established, the relationship carries the request — approval arrives: $35,000 to KAN’s nutrition programme, $12,000 to the early warning system upgrade. Within a week, 800 children under five in the Manu Camp and Northern Coast areas receive supplementary feeding for an additional month. Dr. Ana Tuivaga calls to confirm the first distributions. “This is what was needed,” she says.

The conflict of interest investigation is opened formally, a regional compliance officer assigned. The scoring sheets are audited. The procurement process is found to be sound — no fraud, no preferential pricing — but the undisclosed family connection represented a real governance gap. A revised disclosure policy is developed with Toma and adopted nationally.

The HQ visibility campaign is declined in its original form. Maya calls the regional director and proposes an alternative: a consent-based photography process, community benefit-sharing for participants, credited captions. HQ accepts. The redesigned campaign — signed consent forms, participant copies, community acknowledgement section — is delivered within the original $12,000 budget. It is adopted as a reference model for HopeForward’s global communications policy.

Where community_informed is established, the consent conversation is straightforward: community members understand their rights and engage on their own terms.

CHS 9.1 met — resource gap assessed and addressed. 9.2 met — reallocation pursued through approved channels. 9.3 met — fundraising ethics upheld, consent framework applied. 9.4 met — $47,000 reaches intended community purpose. 9.5 met — conflict of interest investigated formally. 9.6 met — coherent, principled approach to all three issues demonstrated.

scenes.title: Right Goal, Wrong Road
scenes.narrative_summary: The KAN nutrition programme and the early warning upgrade happen. Real children benefit. But the unilateral reallocation surfaces in an audit report. Toma — who flagged the issue correctly — is formally censured because he signed off on the transfer. Maya receives an HQ reprimand. The proper process, which would have taken 48 hours longer, would have achieved the same outcome without institutional damage.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Good intentions do not retrospectively authorise financial management breaches. The process for transparent, approved reallocation exists for good reasons — using it correctly protects everyone.
scenes.chs_quote: Manage financial resources responsibly in line with recognised good practices. (CHS 9.2)
scenes.image_prompt: Split scene. Left panel — month 6: Toma Faleolo signs a transfer authorisation form at his desk, expression determined, a KAN programme brief visible beside the keyboard — the $47,000 reallocation underway, no donor approval email in sight. Right panel — six months later: an external audit office. A compliance officer holds an open audit report, a section circled in red — ‘Unilateral reallocation of restricted donor funds: $47,000.’ Beside her, Toma sits in a chair, formal and still, a letter visible in his hand marked ‘Formal Censure.’ On the far wall, a small KAN nutrition poster — children under five, healthy — a reminder of what the money achieved and what it cost. 16-bit pixel art scene, Pacific island humanitarian setting, muted ochre and grey, right outcome, wrong process, visible cost.
scenes.narrative:
The KAN nutrition allocation — $35,000 — is transferred within 48 hours. The early warning system upgrade — $12,000 — follows the same week. Real children benefit. Dr. Ana Tuivaga confirms the first distributions before month 6 closes. The visibility campaign is declined. The conflict of interest investigation is opened formally and finds no fraud; a revised disclosure policy follows.

The donor is not consulted on the reallocation. Toma signs the transfer authorisation himself, believing the purpose justifies the action and that requesting approval would only delay what is clearly right.

Six months later, a routine audit identifies the unilateral transfer of $47,000 in restricted donor funds without prior approval. The audit report is precise: the reallocation achieved the correct community purpose but violated clause 14.3 of the donor agreement and HopeForward’s financial management policy. Toma — who signed the transfer — is formally censured. He accepts the finding without contesting it. Maya receives an HQ reprimand. HopeForward reimburses the donor $47,000 from organisational reserves.

Where donor_transparent_engagement had been established, the donor would likely have approved the reallocation within 48 hours. The delay Toma was trying to avoid was not the obstacle he assumed it to be.

CHS 9.3 met — fundraising ethics upheld, campaign declined. 9.4 met — funds reach intended community purpose. 9.5 met — conflict of interest formally investigated. 9.2 missed — financial management rules bypassed. 9.6 missed — ethical intent without proper process is not a coherent approach. 9.1 partially met.

scenes.title: The Cost of Looking Away
scenes.narrative_summary: Everything in the file looks clean. Then, one by one: the conflict of interest appears in an external audit; two Kailani community members file an image consent complaint with HopeForward HQ; and a sector media outlet publishes a critical analysis of HopeForward's Kailani visibility campaign, featuring screenshots of the community photos used without consent. The article is shared 2,000 times in the humanitarian sector.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Choosing compliance over stewardship, and choosing to look away from ethical breaches, is a form of active complicity. CHS 9 requires leaders to use resources for communities — not as if communities are resources.
scenes.chs_quote: Ensure fundraising, resource mobilisation, and fund allocations are ethical and do not compromise the organisation's commitments and values. (CHS 9.3)
scenes.image_prompt: Three moments across one year, arranged in a triptych. Left — month 6: a clean programme closure report on a desk, ‘All Financials Reconciled’ stamped, the conflict of interest folder still in the filing cabinet, drawer closed. Centre — month 12: an external audit table, a compliance officer pointing to a highlighted section — ‘Undisclosed procurement relationship identified; internal record found.’ A printout of the Manu Camp family photograph lies beside two formal complaint letters. Right — month 14: a laptop screen showing a published article titled ‘When NGO Fundraising Exploits the Communities It Claims to Serve,’ with a screenshot of the HopeForward donation page bearing the Manu Camp photo; a share count reads ‘2,147.’ 16-bit pixel art scene, Pacific island humanitarian setting, cool grey palette, clean surfaces concealing accumulated cost.
scenes.narrative:
Everything is filed correctly. The $47,000 is returned within 72 hours — donor agreement clause 14.3 satisfied, no follow-up required. The conflict of interest note remains in the filing cabinet. The visibility campaign is approved in full: $12,000, Suva-based agency, the Manu Camp family photograph on the donor report cover and the organisation’s donation appeal page.

The programme closes without incident. The month-6 financial summary is clean.

Then, one by one:

At month 10, a routine external audit surfaces the undisclosed procurement relationship. The auditors find the internal note — written, filed, unacted — and classify it as a governance failure more serious than the original omission. A donor review is convened.

At month 11, two Manu Camp community members file independent complaints with HopeForward HQ: they have seen themselves on a donation appeal page and did not consent. One has a child in the photograph. Where community_informed had been established, they would have known their rights before the campaign launched. The complaints are escalated to global PSEA.

At month 14, a humanitarian sector media outlet publishes a critical analysis of image consent practices across international NGOs. HopeForward’s Kailani visibility campaign is featured by name. The article is shared 2,000 times. Two institutional donors request formal written responses.

All six CHS 9 requirements missed: 9.1 — staffing gap unaddressed; 9.2 — letter met, spirit absent; 9.3 — fundraising ethics breached, community images used without consent; 9.4 — $47,000 returned to donor, not directed to community benefit; 9.5 — conflict of interest deliberately left unmanaged; 9.6 — no coherent ethical approach, only the appearance of compliance.

scenes.title: Your Mission Report
scenes.narrative_summary: Maya sits at the departure gate in Port Hana airport. Six months condensed into a notebook of decisions, conversations, and lessons. She thinks of Sina Talagi's handwritten complaint, Leila's message from a blocked checkpoint, the Tevita village circle that changed the programme's direction, the fishing cooperative's first season after the grant. The score she carries is not a grade — it is a record of every trade-off, every choice to listen or not listen, every moment when principles and pressure pulled in different directions.
scenes.image_prompt: Interior of Port Hana airport departure gate, Kailani. Maya Chen — East Asian woman, mid-thirties, short dark hair, wearing a HopeForward International light-blue field vest over a plain shirt — sits in an airport seat beside a large window. Through the window: Kailani's coastal landscape in late afternoon light, some cyclone damage still visible on the shoreline but green recovery growth visible too. She holds a worn, annotation-filled field notebook in one hand and a boarding pass in the other. Expression: reflective, tired, quietly proud. A departure board above the gate shows her flight. 16-bit pixel art scene style, warm sunset palette — deep ochre, coral, and Pacific blue — consistent Pacific humanitarian field setting.
scenes.narrative:
The departure gate is quiet — a mid-afternoon lull between flights. Maya sits with a coffee she has barely touched, her worn field notebook open on the seat beside her. Six months. A boarding pass for a flight to somewhere else.

She thinks of Sina Talagi's handwritten complaint — five careful pages in neat blue ink, slid under the office door before dawn — and what might have happened if she had filed it away instead of followed it. She thinks of Leila's voice on the satellite phone from a blocked checkpoint on the Northern Coast road: flat with exhaustion, three weeks after the decision Maya made about her safety. She thinks of the Tevita village circle — forty-seven people under a corrugated roof, a programme that was about to miss them entirely — and the moment everything shifted because someone finally asked the right questions. She thinks of the fishing cooperative's first season report, forwarded by Filomena with a short note: *They did this themselves.*

Her score — whatever it is — is not a grade. It is a record of every trade-off she accepted, every choice to listen or not listen, every moment when principles and pressure pulled in different directions and she had to decide who she was.

The gate opens. She closes the notebook, picks up her bag, and boards.

endings.title: Transformative Leader
endings.summary: Maya's leadership in Kailani will be studied as a model of principled, inclusive, locally-led humanitarian action. She listened to marginalised voices, protected her staff, maintained accountability when it was uncomfortable, coordinated generously, used evidence to improve the programme, and left stronger local systems behind. All nine CHS commitments were honoured in practice — not just in policy.
endings.narrative_summary: Maya sits with her notebook at Port Hana departure gate and reads the last entry: 'The committee authorised it. I didn't need to.' From the start she slowed down to hear the community — Tevita speakers, women, people with disabilities, those the survey skipped. She redesigned distribution around real needs rather than pre-approved boxes, embedded local knowledge from day one, and handed governance to Filomena's committee before the first budget variance arrived. She established PSEA and data-protection frameworks before harm could occur, followed Sina's complaint to its full survivor-centred resolution, and opened the cluster data that got Dr. Ana Tuivaga into the room. When Amara's gender gap appeared in the mid-term data, Maya acted — not filed. When Leila called at 3 am, Maya answered. When Toma's conflict-of-interest note arrived, she opened the investigation. All nine CHS commitments honoured in practice, not just in policy. The fishing cooperative's first season report is in the back pocket of the notebook: 'They did this themselves.'
reflection_questions: What made it possible to prioritise community voice even under time and donor pressure?
Which of the nine CHS commitments felt most natural to uphold, and which required the most effort?
What would you do differently in the next response — even after a strong performance?
How did the early decisions shape your options in later chapters?

Gold outer ring with nine small icons evenly spaced around the circumference, each representing one CHS commitment: a speech bubble (participation), a water drop (timely support), a seedling (resilience), a shield (do no harm), a complaint box (accountability), two overlapping circles (coordination), a lightbulb (learning), a person with a heart (staff wellbeing), and a coin with a checkmark (ethical resources). Centre image: a Pacific island coastline in aerial view — green recovery vegetation visible among light cyclone damage, a small fishing cooperative on the shore, a community meeting shelter. Warm late-afternoon Pacific light. Text at base: 'Transformative Leader'. Colour palette: deep gold, ocean blue, fresh green, white. Flat digital illustration style, clean and professional.

endings.title: Committed Practitioner
endings.summary: Maya made principled choices in most situations but faced real trade-offs and sometimes chose pragmatism over principle. Her commitment to quality is visible, and the programme left positive impact. There are specific CHS commitments where the full standard was not met — but the intent was always there. The learning journey continues.
endings.narrative_summary: Maya closes her notebook at the departure gate and lets herself sit with a mixed feeling: satisfaction and an honest accounting of what fell short. She heard communities better than most — but not everyone. The Tevita sessions happened; the disability section was partially reached. Distribution was mostly right, though a few households were served by others' registries without her knowing until week three. She set up a complaints mechanism, but Sina's process felt slower and more uncertain than it should have. She joined the cluster and shared some data, though the full Amara report was not published externally until the donor pressed. Leila was supported — eventually. The fishing cooperative grant was approved, but by Maya, not Filomena. The programme was good. It helped people. Some CHS commitments were met fully; others were met in intent more than practice. That gap is not a verdict. It is the starting point for the next response.
endings.cascading_flags_notes: Some high-value flags set; some missed. Balance of positive and neutral consequences.
reflection_questions: Which decisions were hardest and what made them hard?
Where did operational pressure most consistently pull you away from CHS principles?
Which CHS commitment do you most want to strengthen in your next response?
What context flags did you set — and what would have been different if you had set them differently?
endings.badge_image_prompt: Digital badge, circular format. Silver outer ring. Centre image: a Pacific island outline with visible recovery — new green growth alongside remaining cyclone damage. A figure (representing Maya) stands on a path that leads forward, facing toward the horizon. Around the inner ring: small growth icons — an upward arrow, an open book, a sprouting plant, a handshake. Text at base: 'Committed Practitioner'. Colour palette: silver, teal, warm yellow, white. Flat digital illustration style.

endings.title: Struggling Responder
endings.summary: Maya's response was well-intentioned but inconsistent. Some communities and staff were let down. Operational pressures frequently overrode principled choices. The gap between what CHS requires and what the programme delivered is significant across multiple commitments. Reflecting honestly on those gaps is the first step forward.
endings.narrative_summary: Maya's boarding pass is in her hand. She is not ready to board. The survey skipped the disability section and the Tevita speakers. Distribution happened fast — Carlos's numbers looked clean, but the Tevita household traded HEB packets for water. The programme plan ran without a community committee. When Tropical Storm Beni threatened, there was no protocol. Sina submitted five careful pages and received silence; the complaint box was marked 'no point' in black pen by the following week. The cluster meeting happened, but the data stayed on HopeForward's server. Amara's gender findings were softened in the donor report. Leila's 3 am message waited until morning. The conflict-of-interest note is in a folder marked 'Month 6 Internal Notes.' These were not failures of character. They were failures of system, habit, and pressure — each one manageable, each one a lesson. The next response begins with this one in full view.
endings.cascading_flags_notes: Mostly negative or neutral flags set. Several missed opportunities compounded.
reflection_questions: Which CHS commitments did you find hardest to uphold, and why?
What systemic pressures most affected your decisions — and which of those could you have pushed back against?
What one change in your organisation's systems would help you act differently?
If you replayed this from Decision 1, which single choice would you most want to change — and why?
endings.badge_image_prompt: Digital badge, circular format. Bronze outer ring. Centre image: an open notebook lying on a field table, with a single seedling growing up through the pages — symbolising learning emerging from experience. Faint Pacific island coastal landscape in the background, slightly hazy. Around the inner ring: small icons representing reflection and growth — a magnifying glass, an open hand, a seedling, a looping arrow. Text at base: 'Learning in Progress'. Colour palette: bronze, warm orange, light blue, white. Flat digital illustration style.

endings.title: Crisis Mode
endings.summary: The response in Kailani faced serious setbacks across multiple CHS commitments. Communities were not heard or protected. Staff were harmed. Accountability was compromised. Resources were mismanaged. This is not a verdict on character — it is a signal that operational pressure, institutional incentives, or gaps in knowledge systematically overrode principled practice. The most important step now is honest reflection.
endings.narrative_summary: The departure gate is quiet. Maya stares at the notebook without opening it. The disability section was never reached. Tevita speakers were never heard. Distribution targets were met on paper; actual needs were not. The community committee was never formed. When the data breach happened and the complaint arrived, both were managed for speed, not safety. Sina's five pages of careful writing went unanswered. The cluster stayed closed. Amara's gender data was buried. Leila's 3 am message was forwarded to Carlos. The conflict-of-interest note sits in an internal folder. The visibility campaign ran. Across all nine CHS commitments, the gap between what was required and what was delivered is significant. This is not a verdict on who Maya is. It is a record of what happened when pressure, habit, and institutional incentives were louder than principle. The humanitarian imperative does not expire. It begins again — now, with this accounting in hand.
endings.cascading_flags_notes: Mostly negative flags; cascading harm across multiple chapters.
reflection_questions: Which pressures most consistently pushed you toward less principled choices?
What does accountability mean when you are under-resourced, under pressure, and making decisions alone?
Who in your network could support you to strengthen your practice before the next response?
What would a supportive organisational system have needed to provide for you to make different choices?

A faint Pacific island horizon in the background after a storm: grey-clearing sky, scattered debris, early signs of renewal. Around the inner ring: simple icons of persistence — an upward arrow through a barrier, an open door, a rising sun. Text at base: 'Rising Learner'. key_supporting_characters_expanded.name: Maya Chen
key_supporting_characters_expanded.backstory: Maya grew up in Vancouver, the daughter of Cantonese-speaking immigrants who ran a community health clinic. She trained as a social worker before pivoting to humanitarian action, completing a master's at NOHA and taking her first field posting in the Philippines. Eight years across Asia and East Africa have given her depth — and scar tissue. This is her first Country Director role. She knows the theory and has lived the chaos. What she is still learning is that being right is not the same as building trust.

key_supporting_characters_expanded.name: Sione Taufa
key_supporting_characters_expanded.backstory: Sione is from a neighbouring island, the youngest of seven, and the first in his family to finish university. He studied development studies in Suva on a scholarship and joined HopeForward straight out of graduation. He has spent four years building the relationships and local knowledge that no international hire could replicate. He sees himself as a bridge between his communities and organisations like HopeForward — and he is quietly watching whether Maya is worth bridging to.

key_supporting_characters_expanded.name: Filomena Vaka
key_supporting_characters_expanded.backstory: Filomena has led Kailani Community Voices for eleven years and built it from a small women's savings group into the most respected civil society organisation on the island. She has outlasted four Country Directors from three different INGOs and has seen every variation of 'partnership' that is really just outsourcing. She is warm, sharp, and carefully generous with her trust. What she offers Maya — community access, local legitimacy, institutional memory — is worth more than any consultant's report, and Filomena knows it.

key_supporting_characters_expanded.name: Leila Hassan
key_supporting_characters_expanded.backstory: Leila grew up in Djibouti and took her first humanitarian posting at twenty-two, immediately after her undergraduate degree. She has worked across four countries in six years and chosen the hardest assignments every time. The Northern Coast of Kailani is isolated, under-resourced, and increasingly tense — and Leila took it without complaint. She is meticulous and fearless, which makes her vulnerabilities invisible until a crisis forces them into the light. Her relationship with HopeForward's duty-of-care systems will define whether she stays in the sector.

key_supporting_characters_expanded.name: Carlos Reyes
key_supporting_characters_expanded.backstory: Carlos spent his twenties in supply-chain logistics for a Colombian shipping company before joining the humanitarian sector, drawn in by the 2010 Haiti response. He is one of the most technically competent logistics coordinators HopeForward employs — fast, systematic, and respected by suppliers and drivers. His blind spot is that he treats efficiency as a virtue and community need as a variable in a delivery equation. He means well, but his instinct is always to ask how quickly, not whether.

key_supporting_characters_expanded.name: Amara Diallo
key_supporting_characters_expanded.backstory: Amara studied statistics in Dakar and took a postgraduate diploma in monitoring and evaluation in Nairobi before joining HopeForward three years ago. She is the person in the room who is always the first to ask what the numbers actually mean, and the last to be satisfied with an answer. She cares about learning as a moral act — the point of data is not the donor report; it is the decision it enables. Her partnership with Sione on community feedback data is one of the most productive relationships in the office.

key_supporting_characters_expanded.name: Toma Faleolo
key_supporting_characters_expanded.backstory: Toma is a Kailani national who trained as an accountant with a regional development bank and joined HopeForward eight years ago, turning down a government finance role that would have paid significantly more. He believes in the work. He is also painfully aware that as a national staff member in a senior financial role, every decision he makes is watched more closely than the same decision made by an expatriate colleague. His caution is not timidity. It is professional survival — and it is principled.

key_supporting_characters_expanded.name: Dr. Ana Tuivaga
key_supporting_characters_expanded.backstory: Ana trained as a physician in New Zealand and returned to Kailani to run a district health clinic for a decade before founding the Kailani Aid Network to coordinate local civil society responses. When Cyclone Vera hit, KAN had mobile health teams in the field within six hours — faster than any INGO. Being excluded from the UN cluster is not merely an administrative frustration for Ana; it is a structural indictment of who the international system thinks counts as a legitimate responder. She says this plainly. She is correct.

game_engine_notes.overview: This section provides implementation guidance for developers building a game engine around CHS_2024_game_v2.json. The core mechanic is the context_flags_system: a set of 18 boolean flags, initialised false at game start, set to true by specific option choices, persisted for the full session, and used to conditionally modify situation_summary text and consequence text in later scenes.

context_flags_implementation.storage: Store all 18 flags in a single game-session state object, e.g. { inclusive_assessment: false, community_informed: false, ... }. Initialise all flags to false at game start. Never reset flags mid-session. Flags are set to true when the player selects an option whose sets_flag field matches the flag ID. Flags are never unset.
context_flags_implementation.flag_setting: After the player selects an option and before rendering the consequence scene, evaluate the selected option's sets_flag field (present in the decision option data). Set all listed flags to true in session state. Log flag changes so they can be displayed in the consequence screen's Cascading Note panel.

ui_flag_surfacing.decision_screen_behaviour: At the start of each decision scene, query the session state for all active flags. Check the flag_effects_by_decision map above for which flags affect this decision. If one or more affecting flags are active, display a contextual banner above the situation text: amber background, text reading 'Your earlier choices are shaping this situation.' Below the banner, list each active affecting flag as a chip with its short label. On tap or hover, expand the chip to show the flag's narrative_effect description for this scene.
ui_flag_surfacing.consequence_screen_behaviour: After displaying the score delta, show the 'Cascading Note' panel (amber background) if the chosen option sets a new flag. Display: flag name, a one-sentence description of what it means for the future, and an icon (a small branching-path arrow). This is the moment the player learns a flag has been set — make it clear and memorable.
ui_flag_surfacing.score_dashboard_behaviour: The right panel of the score dashboard lists all 18 flags. Active flags are displayed as filled teal chips with white text. Inactive flags are displayed as empty grey chips with muted text. Chips link to a detail drawer showing the flag's set_by information and effect_summary.
ui_flag_surfacing.ending_screen_behaviour: The ending screen displays all active flags in a dedicated 'Your Journey' section. Each flag is shown with its name and effect_summary. The count of active flags (out of 18) is shown as a summary statistic: e.g. '14 of 18 flags active — you made principled choices in most situations.' This reinforces cumulative learning.
ui_flag_surfacing.flag_persistence: Flags are stored in session state only. If the player uses 'Play Again', all flags reset to false. If the game supports saved sessions (e.g. a web app with localStorage), serialise the full flags object alongside the current scene ID and score state. Always validate the flags object on load — if any flag key is missing, initialise it to false.

rendering_notes.situation_summary_injection: Each decision scene has a situation_summary field. If active flags affect this scene (per the flag_effects_by_decision map), the engine should append or inject contextual text. The recommended approach is to store flag-conditional text as a separate flag_situation_addenda object keyed by flag ID within each scene — allowing clean separation of base text and flag-modified text. For the current skeleton, the narrative_effect descriptions in flag_effects_by_decision above describe what this addendum should convey.
rendering_notes.image_prompt_use: All image_prompt fields are ready for use with AI image generation APIs (DALL-E 3, Midjourney, Stable Diffusion XL, or Adobe Firefly). Pass the prompt string directly. For production, generate and cache images at build time. Store asset paths alongside the game data. All image_prompt fields specify '16-bit pixel art style, 24-colour Kailani palette' to ensure visual coherence across all generated assets. Pass the prompt string directly to your image generation tool of choice.
rendering_notes.chs_badge_rendering: Each decision scene references a CHS commitment number. Render the CHS commitment badge (commitment number and short title) as a persistent element on the decision screen so the learning connection is always visible. The badge should appear even when the player is reading the situation and choosing an option.
rendering_notes.score_delta_animation: When displaying score deltas on the consequence screen, animate each dimension change sequentially (200ms delay between dimensions) to draw attention to the multi-dimensional impact of each choice. Positive deltas: teal fill animation upward. Negative deltas: coral fill animation downward.

pixel_art_implementation.overview: This section provides a pixel art visual style recommendation and engine implementation guide for Kailani Rising. The pixel art approach is an intentional design choice: the lo-fi aesthetic contrasts with the weight of humanitarian subject matter, creating emotional distance that helps learners engage with difficult scenarios without feeling overwhelmed. Retro JRPG and visual-novel conventions are familiar to a wide adult audience and are well-suited to a decision-driven narrative.

recommended_tech_stack.rationale: A vanilla HTML5/JS implementation is the primary recommendation for Kailani Rising. It requires no install, runs in any modern browser, supports full-screen canvas rendering at integer-scaled 320×180 px, GIF sprite animations, CSS transitions for screen changes, and the Web Audio API for chiptune sound. The entire CHS_2024_game_v2.json file is loaded by a single JavaScript fetch() call and parsed into a session-state object. The context_flags_system maps directly to a plain JS object initialised at game start. All pixel art assets are rendered via an HTML5 <canvas> element scaled with CSS (image-rendering: pixelated) to fill the viewport.

alternatives.rationale: Open-source, cross-platform (Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, Web export). Purpose-built for branching narratives with native flag/variable support. A strong choice if web export is not the only target. The JSON can be loaded at runtime with a Python json.loads() block.
Excellent pixel art tooling and a large community asset library. Best if a full overworld map or movement mechanic is desired. Higher visual complexity but requires more development time.
Produces Game Boy-style pixel art games that run in a browser. Extreme accessibility and novelty value. Limited in branching complexity; suitable for a condensed 3-decision version.
Full-featured open-source engine supporting pixel art rendering (nearest-neighbour scaling). Suitable if the game expands beyond a visual novel into a point-and-click or map-navigation format.
Zero-install, runs in any browser. No native pixel art support, but CSS pixel art filters and inline sprite images can achieve the style. Best for rapid prototyping and trainer-editable content.

recommended_tech_stack.data_format_note: CHS_2024_game_v2.json is loaded directly by the JavaScript engine via fetch(). The JSON is parsed into a session-state object: scenes are indexed by ID for O(1) lookup, context flags are initialised as a plain object with all 18 keys set to false, and score dimensions are tracked as four numeric counters. In Ren'Py, the JSON is best converted to a Python dictionary at game start (json.loads()). In Godot, use the built-in JSON class.

ren_py_implementation_sketch.description: Skeleton Ren'Py script structure for loading CHS_2024_game_v2.json and rendering the first decision. Expand this pattern for all nine decisions.
file_structure: game/
script.rpy          — main game flow, scene rendering loop
json_loader.rpy     — Python block to load and parse CHS_2024_game_v2.json
flags.rpy           — flag initialisation and helper functions
characters.rpy      — character definitions and sprite assignments
screens.rpy         — custom screens: decision_screen, consequence_screen, score_dashboard, ending_screen
images/
bg/               — background pixel art (320x180 PNG, nearest-neighbour)
sprites/          — character sprites (48x48 PNG spritesheets, 4 frames)

ren_py_implementation_sketch.script_rpy_sketch:
# script.rpy — simplified skeleton
default flags = {f['id']: False for f in game_data['context_flags_system']['flags']}
default scores = {'community_trust': 0, 'staff_wellbeing': 0, 'program_quality': 0, 'accountability': 0}

label start:
    python:
        import json
        with open(config.gamedir + '/CHS_2024_game_v2.json', 'r') as f:
            game_data = json.load(f)
        scenes_by_id = {s['id']: s for s in game_data['scenes']}
        current_scene_id = 'prologue'
    jump scene_loop

label scene_loop:
    python:
        scene = scenes_by_id[current_scene_id]
    if scene['type'] == 'narrative':
        jump render_narrative
    elif scene['type'] == 'decision':
        jump render_decision
    elif scene['type'] == 'consequence':
        jump render_consequence

label render_decision:
    show screen decision_screen(scene, flags, scores)
    $ chosen_option_id = ui.interact()
    python:
        option = next(o for o in scene['options'] if o['id'] == chosen_option_id)
        for dim, val in option['score_effects'].items():
            scores[dim] += val
        for flag_id in option['sets_flag']:
            flags[flag_id] = True
        current_scene_id = option['consequence_scene_id']
    jump scene_loop

accessibility_notes: Use integer scaling only (1×, 2×, 3×, 4×) — never fractional scaling — to preserve pixel art crispness.
Provide a font-size toggle that switches from pixel font to a system sans-serif (e.g. Atkinson Hyperlegible) for players who find bitmap fonts hard to read.
Ensure all colour-coded score deltas are also labelled with text (do not rely on colour alone — support colour-blind players).
Provide subtitles for any voice-acted content.
Minimum tap target size on mobile: 44x44 CSS pixels, equivalent to at least 22x22 game pixels at 2× scale.
Translate the pixel art option buttons and dialogue boxes into screen-reader-friendly HTML alt text if deploying as a web app.

html_js_implementation_sketch.description: Skeleton HTML/JS structure for a web-based Kailani Rising implementation. The game renders a 320×180 px pixel art canvas that fills the browser viewport using CSS (image-rendering: pixelated; width: 100vmin; height: auto;). GIF animations are used for character idle/talk cycles and scene transitions. All game data is loaded from CHS_2024_game_v2.json via fetch().
file_structure: index.html              — entry point; full-screen canvas mount
style.css               — viewport fill, pixelated rendering, UI overlay styles
js/
main.js               — game loop, scene routing, input handling
loader.js             — fetch() + JSON parse; buildSceneIndex()
flags.js              — initFlags(), setFlag(id), isActive(id)
score.js              — initScores(), applyDelta(dim, val), getTotal()
renderer.js           — drawBackground(), drawSprite(), drawDialogueBox()
ui.js                 — renderDecisionScreen(), renderConsequenceScreen(), renderScoreDashboard(), renderEndingScreen()
audio.js              — Web Audio API chiptune player
assets/
bg/                   — background pixel art (320×180 PNG, nearest-neighbour)
sprites/              — character GIF sprite sheets (48×48 px, 4-frame idle/talk)
ui/                   — UI tile PNGs (buttons, dialogue box, flag chips, badges)
audio/                — chiptune .ogg tracks and .ogg sound effects
data/
CHS_2024_game_v2.json — game data (loaded at runtime)
